posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
I opened a bottle of Julian Barge Clos de la Ribaudy St Joseph tonight, and was surprised to see a 2017 cork after cutting the foil.

I’ve only had a few 2018 Rhônes, but this doesn’t taste like a solar year wine. Elegant, understated, and not close to being overripe. I’m thinking this is likely the 2017 - what’s the likelihood that I’m right?

image1920×2560 399 KB

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked: I think its the 2018. This has happened to me in the past and I was told to go with what the label says.

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked: I am pretty sure it will be the 2018. Sometimes smaller producers use leftover corks.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked: what these guys said, trust the label

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Thanks hive mind! In any case, a terrific St. Joseph that I’ll be looking out for from now on.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
I would have said cork since often labels aren’t put on till
years later when wine gets sold. Cork is of course placed when bottled

posts.name: Tony Gigante
posts.cooked:
Time for a poll
I’m going with the label being correct and the cork being leftover

It would be a fairly large error to mislabel the wine

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Like Alan, I would tend to trust the cork over the label, myself. Bottles are stored unlabelled, and perhaps a bottle somehow got mixed up.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
Get another 18 and see if it is similar
Also a big fan of Barge’s wines even though i only tasted his Cote-Rotie’s. Need to find some Saint-Joseph it seems.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Yeah I’d also say cork over label. Check out this letter I got from Dr. Loosen last year.

Screen Shot 2022-09-12 at 8.51.43 AM1268×1426 84.2 KB
